coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as rapid assays for detection of mercury(II) ions based on turn-off mode and breast milk
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pajewska-Szmyt, Martyna
Buszewski, Bogusław
Gadzała-Kopciuch, Renata
coredata.dc:description: In this research, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots were synthesized by solid thermal method with citric acid used as the precursor of carbon, and melamine as nitrogen source. Such carbon dots show high quantum yield of 44%. Furthermore, the native fluorescence of CDs can be reduced by mercury(II), while other metals had no significant influence on fluorescence intensity. During the study, the optimal parameters were selected, such as pH or time for incubation with analyte. Under the optimal conditions, quenching effect caused by mercury ions was evaluated. It was observed that with increasing mercury concentration, the fluorescence of the carbon dots decreased proportionally. The response was characterized by linearity within the range from 2 to 14 μM. Moreover, the limit of detection was 0.44 μM. It was the first time that human milk was used as a real sample to test the applicability of carbon dots. The study results demonstrated good recovery in the 74–111% range (RSD < 6%) As a novel carbon material, CDs show promise for broader applications in analyzing complicated biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Assays
Mercury(II)
Breast milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence stability of the prepared carbon dots
Application of carbon dots for human milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis (A) and fluorescence spectra (B) of carbon dots synthesized by solid state thermal method.
TEM images of carbon dots and the size distribution of CDs.
FTIR spectra of the prepared carbon dots.
Influence of pH on the quenching effect caused by mercury(II). F0 – native fluorescence of carbon dots, F – fluorescence of carbon dots after incubation with the analyte.
Fluorescence stability of obtained carbon dots for five months.

Fluorescence of carbon dots treated by different Hg(II) concentrations (A) and change of fluorescence of carbon dots in the presence of different metal ions with concentration ten times higher than mercury(II) (B).
Methods tested for milk sample pre-treatment (A) and their effect on fluorescence stability of carbon dots (B).
FL intensity of the carbon dots for different concentrations of mercury(II) in a solution containing human milk.
Examples of carbon dots reported as mercury determination assays, with nitrogen content and quantum yield.
Determination of Hg(II) in matrix containing human milk.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as rapid assays for detection of mercury(II) ions based on turn-off mode and breast milk

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics, Faculty of Chemistry, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, 7 Gagarin St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics, Faculty of Chemistry, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, 7 Gagarin St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics, Faculty of Chemistry, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, 7 Gagarin St., 87-100 Toruń, Poland.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots were synthesized by solid thermal method with citric acid used as the precursor of carbon, and melamine as nitrogen source. Such carbon dots show high quantum yield of 44%. Furthermore, the native fluorescence of CDs can be reduced by mercury(II), while other metals had no significant influence on fluorescence intensity. During the study, the optimal parameters were selected, such as pH or time for incubation with analyte. Under the optimal conditions, quenching effect caused by mercury ions was evaluated. It was observed that with increasing mercury concentration, the fluorescence of the carbon dots decreased proportionally. The response was characterized by linearity within the range from 2 to 14 μM. Moreover, the limit of detection was 0.44 μM. It was the first time that human milk was used as a real sample to test the applicability of carbon dots. The study results demonstrated good recovery in the 74–111% range (RSD < 6%) As a novel carbon material, CDs show promise for broader applications in analyzing complicated biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) is one of the heavy metals that can pose a serious health risk; it has highly thiophilic properties due to its tendency to bond with thiol-containing compounds, which may create an imbalance in a biological system [1]. Formation of strong mercury complexes with amino acids, which are the main building blocks of proteins and enzymes, can cause serious hepatotoxicity and neurotoxicity [2]. Therefore, there is a great interest in new methods for the determination of this toxic metal. Among the various methods that have been proposed to this aim, in the majority use atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [3–6] or mass spectroscopy [7–9]. However, these techniques require complex equipment and qualified personnel to operate them. Moreover, the technique of sample preparation for these methods often requires use of drastic conditions and harmful reagents. All this translates into long and costly analysis, which unfortunately limit wide applications of those methods [10]. The solution to these disadvantages may be fluorescence spectroscopy, which involves novel sensors that combine advantages of selectivity and sensitivity with quick response, low cost-effectiveness and simplicity [11]. In most cases, fluorescent materials used for detection of Hg(II) are based on emission intensity changes as a result of the coordination of heavy metal ions with some functional groups at the sensor surface. The role of a sensor working in this way can be assumed by carbon-based materials such as carbon dots (CDs), which are the smallest member of the carbon nanomaterial family. One of the advantages of carbon dots is that they are simple to make. Two approaches can be used for the synthesis: in a top-down one, macroscopic carbon structures such as carbon nanotubes are cut; in bottom-up approaches, a molecular precursor is needed, in which carbon atoms are present (e.g. citric acid). In most cases, these methods do not involve any surface passivation [12]. The methods used in this technique include ultrasound [13], pyrolysis [14], microwave assisted [15], hydrothermal [16–20] and solid state thermal [21,22]. Quantitative analysis using carbon dots is possible due to the change in their native fluorescence. After the interaction between carbon dots and an analyte, e.g. mercury, the quenching effect is most often observed, but also enhancing can be noticed. Properly selected precursors for synthesis will allow obtaining high selectivity, sensitivity and linearity of the probe i.e. the change in fluorescence will be proportional to the increase of analyte concentration. Therefore, the synthesis of carbon dots is often carried out not only with the carbon source but also with other elements. In view of the above, heteroatom doping is an intriguing approach because of the possibility to obtain selective carbon dots and to enhance their fluorescence properties. The elements introduced to the surface of dots include nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorus and so on, but it is the first one – nitrogen – that is considered as an outstanding element for doping carbon dots [23] due to its valence electrons and atomic size optimal to combine with carbon atoms. The obtained carbon dots can be used as an assay for mercury ions determination, which has already been tried in several works. However, the matrix on which those researchers worked was usually a water sample [15,17,24–29]. The use of a different matrix is much more demanding, as it is often necessary to select an appropriate pre-treatment method for such samples, in particular, because other components of the sample may affect on the performance of the carbon dots, i.e. on its selectivity and limit of detection. Moreover, the method and the reagents used in it can also significantly influence the assays behaviour e.g. by disturbing the stability of carbon dots. Biological samples are the greatest challenge for analysis due to their complex composition. However, the potential use of this type of assays requires research into such complex matrices. It should be noted that attempts are being made to use these carbon dots with such samples as urine [30] or human serum [31]. But there have been no reports where human milk was used as a matrix. It is therefore extremely interesting to investigate the application of carbon dots as a sensor with human milk as the matrix. A sample of this type makes such analysis difficult, mainly due to its high protein content [32]. Therefore, the first step should be the elimination of proteins from the sample. We should not forget either about other compounds from the group of fats, hydrocarbons as well as micro and macroelements, which may affect the intensity of fluorescence and the quenching effect [32]. Therefore, the research described here undertook the synthesis of carbon dots for the determination of mercury: solid state thermal method was applied with citric acid as main carbon source and melamine as a precursor of nitrogen element. In order to investigate the potential applicability, the assay performance was also tested using a human milk sample. As said above, such research on a novel matrix can serve as a basis for the future use of this type of carbon dots in biological samples.

For the synthesis of carbon dots, anhydrous citric acid (Merck, Germany) and melamine (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) were used. The preparation of Britton-Robinson buffer was conducted using sodium hydroxide (POCH, Poland); acetic acid (J.T. Baker, USA); phosphoric(V) acid and boric acid (POCH, Poland); Quantum yield was assigned with quinine sulfate (suitable for fluorescence, Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The following reagents were used for interference study: barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4), manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), mercury(II) chloride (HgCl2); (POCH, Poland). All solutions were prepared with deionized water from a MiliporeMilliQ System (Merck, USA).

The physicochemical and optical properties were evaluated with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Tecnai F20 X-Twin (FEI Europe), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Vertec 70V), elementary analysis (Vario Macro CHN), fluorescence spectroscopy (Jasco FP-8300, Europe) and UV–Vis spectrometer (NanoDrop ND2000, Thermo Scientific, USA).

The carbon dots were synthesized by bottom up approaches using one step solid thermal method. The synthesis was performed according to the procedure described by Iqbal et al. [21]. For this purpose anhydrous citric acid (CA) and melamine (ME) were used as the main carbon and doping element source, respectively. Both reagents (0.4 g CA and 0.8 g ME, respectively) were transferred into a reactor (50 mL teflon lined autoclave). Subsequently, the content of the reactor was heated to 180 ̊C and kept at this temperature for 5 h. After this time, reactor was left to cool down to room temperature overnight. The obtained black solid product was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. To remove impurities, the solution of carbon dots was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filter.

The obtained carbon dots solution has been diluted 10 times in Briton-Robinson Buffer (pH = 7) and incubated for 30 min to stabilize the native fluorescence of carbon dots. Then series of mercury(II) ions standard solutions were prepared and 100 μL of as-prepared carbon dots solution was added to it. The final volume of the mixture was 4 mL. The content of the tube was shaken without light access for 30 min. After this time, the fluorescence spectra were recorded, using wavelength excitation at 352 nm and emission at 427 nm.

In order to check the selectivity of the obtained carbon dots, it is important to check their sensitivity to other metals than analyte. Therefore, a series of solutions containing particular metals in buffer were prepared: Hg2+,Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Fe3+. The mixture of standard solution of mercury(II) ions was prepared at the level 2.5 μM and other metals with concentration ten times higher than mercury(II). The fluorescence of the obtained dots was then measured without the addition of a metal standard (F0) and with the addition of the appropriate metal, after 30 min of incubation (F).

Breast milk was used to evaluate the practical application of the carbon dots assay. 5 mL of breast milk was transferred into an Amicon filter tube (3kD) to remove proteins, which was followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 60 min. The obtained extract was transferred to another tube and diluted. After that, different concentrations of Hg(II) were added to it (0.3–102.1 μM). The next step was to introduce into this solution 100 μL of prepared carbon dots and incubate for 30 min. Finally, the intensity of carbon dots fluorescence in breast milk extract was recorded.

The optical properties of carbon dots were evaluated by UV–Vis absorption and FL spectra. The absorption spectra show peaks at 215 and 230 nm, which can be connected with the π-π* transition [33,34]. Moreover, broad absorption peak at 330 nm might be ascribed to n-π transition of CO bond (Fig. 1A) [21,33]. The optimal excitation wavelength was 352 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was 427 nm (Fig. 1B). These parameters were selected as optimal for further verification and optimization of the procedure. The obtained carbon dots are characterized by high quantum yield (Table 1 ), which was recorded to be 44%. This result is slightly higher than that obtained by Iqbal et al. (40%) [21].

Table 2 shows examples of noted quantum dots, taking into account the obtained quantum yield. It should be pointed out that the quantum yield In many studies is around 20% [15,17,35]. However, the result obtained from these study is noticeably higher than in the other works, if we consider for example, carbon dots synthesized by Wang et al. with the hydrothermal method using triethylamnine as source of nitrogen elements, which are characterized by quantum yield as 18.8% [34], or 12.3% QY of carbon dots prepared with sodium salicylate and ethylendiamine [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of the prepared carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the quenching effect caused by mercury, a set of standard solutions has been prepared (0.27–70 μM). According to the procedure described in Section 2.4, fluorescence intensity for carbon dots was investigated through incubation with mercury ions. As can be seen in Fig. 6A, native FL intensity is decreasing with the increase of toxic metal concentration. Moreover, the recorded limit of the detection was 0.44 μM and the limit of quantification was 1.33 μM. Consequently, linearity was established to be between 2 μM and 14 μM (y = 0.023 + 1.035; R2 = 0.996). The selectivity of the obtained dots was investigated in the presence of ions of other metals (2.5). These results are presented in Fig. 6B, which demonstrates that the quenching effect is the highest for mercury, while in the case of other interferences the change of native fluorescence is not significant. The numbers for the limit of detection as well as selectivity are desirable and show potential for detection of mercury(II) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots for human milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The literature reports on carbon dots and their potential applications suggest that it is possible to use this type of assay with real samples. However, what served as real sample in most cases was tap water (Table 2), a matrix that is simple in comparison with biological ones. Thus the application of carbon dots to determine mercury(II) ions in such biological sample as milk is challenging. Moreover, there have been few cases of applications of this type of sensors to biological samples and the authors had not found any reports on the use of carbon dots in the determination of mercury or other metals in human milk. It should be noted that human milk is an extremely rich matrix that contains many compounds, so the sensor will be affected not only by proteins but also by numerous fats and other macro- and microelements. Therefore, determination of mercury ions in such matrix requires an appropriate sample pre-treatment method. Considering that, numerous approaches were investigated with a view to selecting the method: (1) protein precipitation by acetonitrile (ACN) and salts as anhydrous magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) and sodium acetate (CH3COONa); (2) protein precipitation by trichloroacetic acid (TCA) (1% or 20%); (3) protein removal with an Amicon filter tube (3 kDa) (Fig. 7A).

Carbon dots are a new type of materials which can be used as a receptor in detection of heavy metals such as mercury. In summary, fluorescence assay was successfully synthesized with citric acid and melamine by a variant of the thermal method. The characteristics of as-prepared carbon dots was compiled with instrumental techniques, which provided essential information about their structure and optical properties. These results made it possible to use the as-prepared carbon dots to determine the mercury that caused the quenching of their native fluorescence. The applicability of CDs was tested for real samples like human milk, which was challenging due to the complexity of the matrix. This research showed how important it is to have a suitable method for pre-treatment of the sample. It should be noted that solutions using an organic reagent or acid resulted in a very low stability of the dots, which consequently limited their application. In turn, after purification with an Amicon filter tube it was possible to use the obtained human milk matrix for testing the application potential of the assay. Consequently, this study developed an easy strategy for identification of mercury ions and demonstrated its applicability for the analysis of biological samples.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Martyna Pajewska-Szmyt: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Bogusław Buszewski: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Renata Gadzała-Kopciuch: Resources, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the National Science Centre within the framework of the project OPUS No. 2018/29/B/ST4/01681.
